content.text: Next-token prediction is the training objective and inference mechanism that underlies every autoregressive language model — GPT, LLaMA, Claude, Gemini, and their kin. The model receives a sequence of tokens (subword units derived from raw text) and must predict the probability distribution over the vocabulary for the very next token. That single, deceptively simple task, applied to a corpus of hundreds of billions of tokens, causes a neural network to internally develop representations of grammar, fact, reasoning, and style — without any explicit labeling.
, the model parameterized by θ outputs

— a probability distribution over the vocabulary V. At training time, the correct next token is known (it is the next token in the text), so the objective is to maximize the log-probability assigned to the actual next token. At inference time, a token is sampled (or the most likely one is selected) from this distribution, appended to the context, and the process repeats — this is the autoregressive loop.

The vocabulary typically ranges from 32,000 (LLaMA) to 200,000+ (GPT-4 family) tokens. These tokens are not characters or words but subword units produced by a tokenizer (Byte-Pair Encoding or SentencePiece), calibrated to give common words a single token while splitting rare words into components. So "unbelievable" might be tokenized as ["un", "believ", "able"], and each step predicts the next subword unit in that chain.

Next-token prediction is the universal pre-training objective of the LLM era. Its elegance is that it is entirely self-supervised: no human labels are needed, only raw text. This means the training set can be arbitrarily large — the internet, books, code repositories, scientific papers — and the model learns from all of it simultaneously. Alternative objectives (masked language modeling as in BERT, seq2seq as in T5) require different architectures and have different strengths, but autoregressive next-token prediction has proven to scale most effectively to the largest models.

Understanding this mechanism is foundational for anyone building with LLMs. Every failure mode — hallucination, repetition, context confusion, prompt sensitivity — has roots in the autoregressive generation process. Knowing that a model is trained to predict the statistically likely next token (not to retrieve facts from a knowledge base, not to reason from first principles) frames why it sometimes confidently produces plausible-sounding nonsense and why its outputs are sensitive to framing and word choice.

For agentic AI systems specifically, next-token prediction imposes a serial constraint on inference: each token must be generated sequentially, since generating token t+1 requires token t. This is why latency scales with output length, and why speculative decoding, parallel decoding, and streaming are active research areas for agentic workloads where responsiveness matters.

Think of the model as a text-completion engine with enormous context awareness, not as a knowledge database. When you prompt a model, you are extending a document, and the model continues it in the style and substance that the training data suggests is most likely to follow. The model is not "thinking" about your question in the way a person would — it is, at each step, asking: "given everything in the context window so far, what subword token is most likely to come next?"

A helpful analogy: autocomplete on your phone, but trained on the entirety of human written knowledge, calibrated through RLHF to produce responses a human would rate highly, and with a context window of 100,000+ tokens. The autocomplete analogy breaks down where the behavior becomes complex and coherent across thousands of tokens, but the mechanism — predict the next token given prior context — is exactly the same.

Another model: think of a joint probability distribution over sequences. Training maximizes log P(token sequence) = Σ log P(xₜ | x₁..xₜ₋₁). A perfect model would assign probability 1 to every actual next token in the training corpus. In practice, the model spreads probability over many plausible continuations, and the sampling strategy you choose at inference time (temperature, top-k, top-p) determines how you draw from that distribution.
No annotation required. Any raw text is a valid training example, giving access to effectively unlimited data from the web, books, code, and scientific literature.

The same loss function applies to all languages, domains, and task formats. A model trained on next-token prediction can generate, summarize, classify, translate, and reason — all from the same weights.

Chinchilla scaling laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022) and earlier work by Kaplan et al. (2020) show that validation loss on the next-token objective decreases predictably as a power law with compute, data, and model size. This makes training budget decisions principled.
SFT and RLHF build on the same model weights. The pre-training objective provides a rich initialization for these later stages.
Tokens are emitted one at a time, making it natural to stream partial responses to users as generation proceeds.
Next-token prediction has been applied to Transformers, RNNs, state space models (Mamba), and hybrids. The objective is independent of architecture.

Each token requires a full forward pass through the model, and tokens must be generated sequentially. Generating 1,000 tokens takes 1,000 forward passes. This makes latency proportional to output length.

The model is trained to predict likely tokens, not accurate ones. A model that assigns 80% probability to a false statement will generate it frequently when it is statistically consistent with the context. Accuracy requires additional mechanisms (retrieval, RLHF with fact-checking rewards, tool use).

The model can only condition on tokens within its context window (4k–200k tokens depending on model). Information outside the window is invisible. Long documents must be truncated, chunked, or summarized.

At training time, the model conditions on ground-truth previous tokens. At inference, it conditions on its own previous predictions, which may contain errors. This mismatch (exposure bias) can cause error accumulation in long generations.

The tokenizer imposes a particular segmentation of text. Arithmetic on multi-character numbers, unusual spellings, code in non-Latin scripts, and byte-level nuances can confuse models because the token boundaries don't align with the semantic units.
Very large models require careful hyperparameter tuning, gradient clipping, mixed precision, and often specialized optimizers to train without loss spikes or divergence.

Text is first converted to a sequence of integer token IDs by a tokenizer. BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) starts with individual bytes and iteratively merges the most frequent pair into a new token, producing a vocabulary of 32k–100k+ tokens. The string "the cat sat" might become token IDs [1, 2840, 6798] depending on the vocabulary. The tokenizer also produces special tokens like BOS (beginning of sequence), EOS (end of sequence), and padding tokens used during batched training.

Each token ID is looked up in an embedding matrix E ∈ R^(V × d_model), converting it to a dense vector of dimension d_model (e.g., 4096 for LLaMA 2 7B). A positional encoding is added (or used via rotary positional embeddings like RoPE to encode position relatively) so the model knows which position in the sequence each token occupies.

The sequence of embeddings passes through N Transformer decoder layers. Each layer applies causal (masked) multi-head self-attention — where position t can attend only to positions 1 through t — followed by a position-wise feed-forward sublayer. Layer normalization (pre-norm or post-norm) and residual connections stabilize training. The output of the final layer is a hidden state hₜ ∈ R^d_model for each position t.

The hidden state hₜ is multiplied by the language model head matrix W_lm ∈ R^(d_model × V) (often tied to the embedding matrix E to reduce parameters) to produce logits lₜ ∈ R^V. The softmax over these logits gives the probability distribution: P(xₜ₊₁ | x₁..xₜ) = softmax(lₜ).

The training loss is the mean negative log-likelihood of the ground-truth next tokens across all positions in all training examples. If the correct token at position t is yₜ, then: L = -(1/T) Σₜ log P(yₜ | x₁..xₜ₋₁). Because the Transformer processes all positions in parallel during training (using causal masking to prevent future tokens from leaking), the entire loss for a sequence of T tokens is computed in a single forward pass — this is the key efficiency advantage over sequential models.
The diagram below illustrates the full next-token prediction pipeline: from raw input tokens through the Transformer to a probability distribution and back into the autoregressive loop.

attrs.alt: Next-token prediction pipeline: autoregressive loop from input tokens through Transformer to probability distribution, and training cross-entropy loss

content.text: The architecture separates into two phases. During training, all T next-token predictions are computed simultaneously via the causal mask — position t sees tokens 1..t-1, predicts token t, and the loss at each position contributes to the total. During inference, generation is strictly sequential: token t+1 is sampled from the model's distribution, appended to the context, and the next forward pass begins.

The key architectural constraint is the causal (lower-triangular) attention mask: entry (i, j) of the attention weight matrix is zero whenever j > i. This prevents future tokens from influencing current predictions, which is essential for valid autoregressive modeling. Without this mask, the model could trivially learn to "predict" each token by attending to it directly rather than learning to model the distribution.
Use next-token prediction as your framing when:
Designing pre-training objectives for new language model architectures — this is the baseline to beat.
Interpreting model behavior: asking why a model outputs something is often answered by asking what token sequence the training distribution made most likely given that context.
Debugging generation quality issues — repetition loops, topic drift, and premature termination all have root causes in the autoregressive mechanism.
Evaluating models via perplexity (exp(mean cross-entropy loss)) — lower perplexity means better next-token predictors and generally correlates with downstream task performance.
Building systems that require token-level control (streaming, early stopping, constrained decoding, grammar-guided generation) — all operate at the level of next-token distributions.
You need factual accuracy guaranteed at inference time — retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) or tool calls to authoritative sources are required on top of next-token prediction.

You need parallel decoding (e.g., fill-in-the-middle, masked language modeling) — architectures like BERT predict multiple positions simultaneously, which is incompatible with purely autoregressive generation.
Deterministic outputs are required — autoregressive sampling introduces non-determinism. Use temperature=0 (greedy decoding) and fixed seed to minimize variance.
The following is a complete, minimal implementation of next-token prediction training from scratch using PyTorch. It includes the tokenizer interface, model definition, training loop, and inference:

import math
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader

# ── Minimal character-level tokenizer ───────────────────────────────────────
class CharTokenizer:
    def __init__(self, text: str):
        self.chars = sorted(set(text))
        self.vocab_size = len(self.chars)
        self.stoi = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(self.chars)}
        self.itos = {i: c for i, c in enumerate(self.chars)}

    def encode(self, text: str) -> list[int]:
        return [self.stoi[c] for c in text]

    def decode(self, ids: list[int]) -> str:
        return "".join(self.itos[i] for i in ids)


# ── Dataset: sliding window over token sequence ──────────────────────────────
class NextTokenDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, token_ids: list[int], block_size: int):
        self.data = torch.tensor(token_ids, dtype=torch.long)
        self.block_size = block_size

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data) - self.block_size

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        chunk = self.data[idx : idx + self.block_size + 1]
        x = chunk[:-1]  # input context
        y = chunk[1:]   # target: one position ahead
        return x, y


# ── Causal self-attention ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, n_heads: int, block_size: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % n_heads == 0
        self.h = n_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // n_heads
        self.qkv = nn.Linear(d_model, 3 * d_model, bias=False)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        # Fixed causal mask (buffer, not a parameter)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(block_size, block_size), diagonal=1).bool()
        self.register_buffer("mask", mask)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q, k, v = self.qkv(x).split(C, dim=2)
        def reshape(t):
            return t.view(B, T, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        q, k, v = reshape(q), reshape(k), reshape(v)
        scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        scores = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale
        scores = scores.masked_fill(self.mask[:T, :T][None, None], float("-inf"))
        attn = self.drop(scores.softmax(dim=-1))
        out = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, C)
        return self.proj(out)


# ── Transformer block ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, n_heads: int, block_size: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.ln1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention(d_model, n_heads, block_size, dropout)
        self.ln2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, 4 * d_model),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(4 * d_model, d_model),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.ln1(x))   # residual around attention
        x = x + self.ff(self.ln2(x))     # residual around feed-forward
        return x


# ── Minimal GPT-style language model ─────────────────────────────────────────
class TinyGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, d_model: int, n_heads: int, n_layers: int, block_size: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.block_size = block_size
        self.tok_emb = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        self.pos_emb = nn.Embedding(block_size, d_model)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(0.1)
        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(*[
            TransformerBlock(d_model, n_heads, block_size) for _ in range(n_layers)
        ])
        self.ln_f = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size, bias=False)
        # Weight tying: share token embedding and LM head weights
        self.lm_head.weight = self.tok_emb.weight

    def forward(self, idx, targets=None):
        B, T = idx.shape
        assert T <= self.block_size
        pos = torch.arange(T, device=idx.device)
        x = self.drop(self.tok_emb(idx) + self.pos_emb(pos))
        x = self.blocks(x)
        x = self.ln_f(x)
        logits = self.lm_head(x)          # (B, T, vocab_size)

        loss = None
        if targets is not None:
            # Flatten to (B*T, vocab_size) for cross-entropy
            loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, logits.size(-1)), targets.view(-1))
        return logits, loss

    @torch.no_grad()
    def generate(self, idx, max_new_tokens: int, temperature: float = 1.0) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Autoregressive generation: sample one token at a time."""
        for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
            # Crop context to block_size
            idx_cond = idx[:, -self.block_size:]
            logits, _ = self(idx_cond)
            # Take logits for the last position only
            logits = logits[:, -1, :] / temperature
            probs = logits.softmax(dim=-1)
            next_tok = torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)
            idx = torch.cat([idx, next_tok], dim=1)
        return idx


# ── Training script ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Tiny training corpus
    text = open("input.txt").read() if __import__("os").path.exists("input.txt") else (
        "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. " * 500
    )

    tokenizer = CharTokenizer(text)
    token_ids = tokenizer.encode(text)

    BLOCK_SIZE = 64
    dataset = NextTokenDataset(token_ids, BLOCK_SIZE)
    loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)

    model = TinyGPT(
        vocab_size=tokenizer.vocab_size,
        d_model=128, n_heads=4, n_layers=4,
        block_size=BLOCK_SIZE,
    )
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4, weight_decay=0.1)

    model.train()
    for epoch in range(5):
        total_loss = 0.0
        for x, y in loader:
            _, loss = model(x, y)
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
            optimizer.step()
            total_loss += loss.item()
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: avg loss = {total_loss / len(loader):.4f}")

    # Generate from a seed prompt
    model.eval()
    seed = "The "
    seed_ids = torch.tensor([tokenizer.encode(seed)], dtype=torch.long)
    generated_ids = model.generate(seed_ids, max_new_tokens=100, temperature=0.8)
    print("Generated:", tokenizer.decode(generated_ids[0].tolist()))
Example 1 — Inspecting next-token probabilities with a pre-trained model
This example shows how to extract the raw probability distribution over the vocabulary at each position, giving direct insight into what the model "thinks" the next token should be:

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

model_name = "gpt2"  # use any HuggingFace causal LM
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)
model.eval()

prompt = "The capital of France is"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# outputs.logits shape: (batch=1, seq_len, vocab_size)
logits = outputs.logits  # (1, T, V)
last_logits = logits[0, -1, :]         # logits at last position → next token dist
probs = last_logits.softmax(dim=-1)

# Top-10 next tokens
top_k = 10
top_probs, top_ids = probs.topk(top_k)
print("Top next-token predictions:")
for prob, tok_id in zip(top_probs, top_ids):
    token_str = repr(tokenizer.decode([tok_id.item()]))
    print(f"  {token_str:<20} {prob.item():.4f}")

# Expected output (approximate):
#   ' Paris'              0.7213
#   ' the'                0.0412
#   ' London'             0.0201
#   ...

Perplexity is exp(mean cross-entropy loss) over a test corpus. Lower perplexity means the model assigns higher probability to actual next tokens. This is the primary metric for evaluating pre-trained language models on held-out text:

import math
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

def compute_perplexity(text: str, model_name: str = "gpt2") -> float:
    """Compute perplexity of `text` under model `model_name`."""
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name).eval()

    encodings = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")
    input_ids = encodings.input_ids  # (1, T)
    T = input_ids.size(1)

    # Split into overlapping windows if text is longer than context window
    stride = 512
    max_length = model.config.max_position_embeddings  # e.g. 1024 for GPT-2
    nlls = []
    prev_end_loc = 0

    for begin_loc in range(0, T, stride):
        end_loc = min(begin_loc + max_length, T)
        trg_len = end_loc - prev_end_loc  # number of NEW tokens this window
        input_chunk = input_ids[:, begin_loc:end_loc]
        target_chunk = input_chunk.clone()
        # Only compute loss on the new tokens (not the overlapping context)
        target_chunk[:, :-trg_len] = -100  # -100 is ignored by cross_entropy

        with torch.no_grad():
            outputs = model(input_chunk, labels=target_chunk)
            nll = outputs.loss  # mean NLL over non-ignored tokens

        nlls.append(nll * trg_len)  # weight by number of predicted tokens
        prev_end_loc = end_loc
        if end_loc == T:
            break

    ppl = math.exp(sum(nlls).item() / T)
    return ppl


sample_text = """
Natural language processing has made remarkable strides in recent years.
Large language models trained on next-token prediction can now generate
coherent text, answer questions, write code, and perform complex reasoning.
"""
ppl = compute_perplexity(sample_text.strip())
print(f"GPT-2 perplexity on sample text: {ppl:.2f}")
# A well-known, grammatical English text will score ~30-80 perplexity under GPT-2
# A random character sequence would score thousands
This shows how different decoding strategies draw from the same next-token probability distribution to produce different output behaviors:

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model.eval()

prompt = "Once upon a time in a land far away,"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

# 1. Greedy decoding: always pick the most likely next token
greedy_output = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=50,
    do_sample=False,    # argmax at each step
)
print("Greedy:")
print(tokenizer.decode(greedy_output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

# 2. Temperature sampling: scale logits before softmax, more diverse
temp_output = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=50,
    do_sample=True,
    temperature=0.8,    # < 1.0 sharpens distribution; > 1.0 flattens it
)
print("\nTemperature=0.8 sampling:")
print(tokenizer.decode(temp_output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

# 3. Nucleus (top-p) sampling: sample from the smallest set of tokens
#    whose cumulative probability exceeds p
topp_output = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=50,
    do_sample=True,
    top_p=0.9,          # nucleus containing 90% of probability mass
    temperature=1.0,
)
print("\nTop-p=0.9 sampling:")
print(tokenizer.decode(topp_output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

The karpathy/nanoGPT repository is the canonical minimal implementation of next-token prediction training on the Shakespeare dataset. It trains a GPT-2-scale model from scratch in roughly 15 minutes on an A100 GPU, achieving ~1.49 bits-per-character on the validation set. The key design decisions in nanoGPT directly reflect production LLM training:
The embedding matrix and the LM head share weights. This reduces parameters by V × d_model (≈25M for GPT-2) and is used in virtually every production LLM.
L2 regularization is applied to weight matrices but not to biases or normalization parameters — a critical distinction that naive L2 regularization misses.
Prevents occasional large gradient spikes (common early in training when the model's predictions are far from the targets) from destabilizing the loss landscape.
Linear warmup for the first few hundred steps (to let the Adam optimizer accumulate reliable gradient statistics before taking large steps), followed by cosine decay to a small minimum LR.
Batch token count instead of batch sequence count:
The effective batch size is measured in tokens (e.g., 0.5M tokens/step), and variable-length sequences are packed into fixed-size batches to maximize GPU utilization.

# Condensed training loop from nanoGPT (illustrative, not identical to original)
import torch, math

def get_lr(step, warmup_steps=100, max_steps=2000, max_lr=6e-4, min_lr=6e-5):
    """Cosine decay with linear warmup."""
    if step < warmup_steps:
        return max_lr * step / warmup_steps
    if step > max_steps:
        return min_lr
    decay_ratio = (step - warmup_steps) / (max_steps - warmup_steps)
    coeff = 0.5 * (1.0 + math.cos(math.pi * decay_ratio))
    return min_lr + coeff * (max_lr - min_lr)

# In training loop:
# for step, (x, y) in enumerate(loader):
#     lr = get_lr(step)
#     for param_group in optimizer.param_groups:
#         param_group["lr"] = lr
#     logits, loss = model(x, y)
#     optimizer.zero_grad()
#     loss.backward()
#     torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)  # gradient clip
#     optimizer.step()
#     if step % 100 == 0:
#         print(f"step {step}: loss {loss.item():.4f}, lr {lr:.2e}")
Mistake 1 — Using bidirectional attention for autoregressive generation

If you forget the causal mask, the model can attend to future tokens during training. It then trivially predicts each token by attending to it directly, learning nothing useful about the underlying distribution. The loss appears extremely low during training but the model produces garbage at inference time because future tokens are no longer available.

# WRONG: no causal mask — model cheats by attending to future tokens
scores = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale
probs = scores.softmax(dim=-1)  # each position sees all other positions

# CORRECT: apply upper-triangular mask before softmax
T = q.size(2)
mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=q.device), diagonal=1).bool()
scores = scores.masked_fill(mask[None, None], float("-inf"))
probs = scores.softmax(dim=-1)
Mistake 2 — Not shifting targets by one position

The target for position t is the token at position t+1. A common mistake when constructing the training dataset is aligning inputs and targets at the same position, effectively training the model to predict the current token from itself. The loss will be very low, but no learning occurs.

# WRONG: targets same as inputs
x = tokens[:, :-1]
y = tokens[:, :-1]  # BUG: should be tokens[:, 1:]

# CORRECT: targets are inputs shifted left by one
x = tokens[:, :-1]  # context: all tokens except last
y = tokens[:, 1:]   # target: all tokens except first
                     # x[t] should predict y[t] = tokens[t+1]
Mistake 3 — Ignoring padding tokens in loss

When batching sequences of different lengths with padding, the loss must not include cross-entropy from padding positions. Computing loss over padding positions injects meaningless gradient signal and degrades model quality. The standard approach is to set target token IDs to -100 at padding positions, which PyTorch's cross_entropy ignores.

import torch.nn.functional as F

# WRONG: loss includes padding positions
loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets.view(-1))

# CORRECT: mask padding positions with -100 (ignored by cross_entropy)
# Assuming padding_token_id = 0
targets_masked = targets.clone()
targets_masked[targets == padding_token_id] = -100
loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, vocab_size), targets_masked.view(-1))
Use gradient checkpointing for large models:

Trading compute for memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them. Enabled with model.gradient_checkpointing_enable() in HuggingFace or torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint_sequential.

Rather than padding every sequence to the maximum length, pack multiple short sequences into a single batch slot separated by an EOS token. This avoids wasted computation on padding and maximizes GPU utilization.
Use bfloat16 or float16 during training:

Mixed precision training (torch.cuda.amp) cuts memory roughly in half and speeds up matrix multiplications on Ampere+ GPUs. bf16 is preferred because it has the same dynamic range as float32 (just lower mantissa precision), making it more numerically stable.
Tie embedding weights to the LM head:
lm_head.weight = tok_emb.weight reduces parameters and often improves performance. This forces the model to maintain consistent representations of tokens in both input and output space.

Perplexity (exp(loss)) is the standard metric for comparing language models across different training setups and is more interpretable — a perplexity of 10 means the model is as confused as if it had to choose uniformly among 10 options at each step.
Evaluate with stride for long texts:
When computing perplexity on documents longer than the context window, use overlapping windows with stride to avoid penalizing tokens at the start of each window that have insufficient context.
A gradient norm that grows over time or spikes dramatically signals training instability. Clip gradients (typically to 1.0) and log the norm before clipping to detect these events.

The computational cost of a forward pass through a Transformer LM scales as O(T × d_model²) for the feed-forward layers and O(T² × d_model) for the attention layers. At typical context lengths (T=2048) and model sizes (d_model=4096), the feed-forward cost dominates. At very long contexts (T=32k+), attention becomes the bottleneck.

The Chinchilla scaling law provides an empirically validated relationship: for a model with N parameters, the optimal training token count is approximately 20×N. Models trained on more tokens than this improve continuously but with diminishing marginal returns. Models trained on fewer tokens are compute-suboptimal.

Speculative decoding (Chen et al., 2023) addresses the serial inference bottleneck by using a small draft model to generate k candidate tokens in parallel, which a larger verifier model then accepts or rejects in a single parallel forward pass. This achieves 2–3x throughput improvement with identical output distribution, and is now used in production by multiple major LLM providers.
The standard library for working with pre-trained LLMs. AutoModelForCausalLM loads any GPT-style model, generate() handles all decoding strategies, and Trainer handles distributed training.
The minimal, pedagogically clean implementation of GPT-style next-token prediction. Approximately 300 lines of pure PyTorch, extensively commented.
High-throughput serving library for LLMs. Uses PagedAttention for efficient KV cache management to maximize batch size and throughput. Key optimization for production deployment.
Lightning-based implementation of multiple model architectures (LLaMA, Mistral, Phi) with pre-training, fine-tuning, and quantization support in a single codebase.
NVIDIA's framework for training very large models with tensor parallelism, pipeline parallelism, and sequence parallelism at scale.
IO-aware exact attention algorithm that reduces memory from O(T²) to O(T) and speeds up training by 2-4x. Integrated into PyTorch via F.scaled_dot_product_attention.
Next-token prediction is one of several language modeling objectives. The choice of objective shapes what the model learns and how it can be used:
Cannot see future context; serial inference
Not naturally generative; requires decoder for seq2seq
Slow inference (many denoising steps); less established
Weaker at in-context learning than Transformers

The dominance of next-token prediction for large models comes from a combination of simplicity (no architectural split required), scalability (single loss signal that improves predictably with compute), and versatility (generation, classification, and reasoning all emerge from the same weights).

Because the model predicts the most likely next token given context, an adversary can craft inputs that push the distribution toward harmful completions by exploiting statistical regularities in the training data. Adversarial prefixes — even those that appear benign to humans — can dramatically shift generation behavior. Output filtering, instruction hierarchy enforcement, and constitutional AI techniques address this at the generation level, but the root cause is in the next-token prediction objective itself.

Even with identical inputs and temperature=0 (greedy decoding), outputs may not be fully deterministic across GPU types, CUDA versions, or batch sizes due to floating-point non-associativity in parallel reductions. For reproducibility in research, log both the model checkpoint hash and the exact inference configuration. For production systems that require deterministic outputs, use CPU inference or accept small variance.

In RAG systems and agent workflows, retrieved content or tool call results are inserted into the context window and condition all subsequent next-token predictions. A malicious document can inject instructions that override system prompts. Defense requires treating retrieved context as untrusted data, using separate context sections with explicit priority, and validating outputs against expected schemas rather than relying on the model to reject adversarial context.

Test set contamination — where evaluation benchmarks appear in the training corpus — inflates measured capabilities. The model is not generalizing; it is completing memorized sequences. When evaluating models on public benchmarks, check training data deduplication and cutoff dates to assess contamination risk.

Next-token prediction is the self-supervised objective that powers all autoregressive LLMs: the model learns P(xₜ₊₁ | x₁..xₜ) from raw text with no human labels, achieving broad capabilities as a byproduct.

Training is parallel: the causal mask allows all T next-token predictions for a sequence to be computed in a single forward pass. Inference is serial: each token requires a full forward pass, making output latency proportional to output length.

The loss is cross-entropy averaged over all positions. Perplexity (exp(loss)) is the standard evaluation metric: lower is better, and production-quality models on English text typically achieve single-digit perplexity on held-out corpora.

Hallucination is a direct consequence: the model predicts statistically likely continuations, not factually verified ones. Plausible-but-wrong completions are high-probability outputs under the training objective.
Weight tying (lm_head.weight = tok_emb.weight), gradient clipping, cosine LR scheduling, and AdamW with decoupled weight decay are universal best practices in next-token prediction training.
Speculative decoding uses a small draft model to generate candidate tokens in parallel, then a large model verifies them in one pass — achieving 2-3x throughput with identical output distribution.
The causal mask is non-negotiable: forgetting it causes the model to cheat during training and produce garbage at inference time, when future tokens are unavailable.

Shifting targets by one position (x = tokens[:-1], y = tokens[1:]) is the fundamental dataset construction step for next-token prediction. Every training example teaches the model what comes after every subsequence of text.
